narrative_summary:
The BRCA1 c.1486C>T (p.Arg496Cys, R496C) missense change has been identified in a range of genetic testing contexts, most often in archival or germline sequencing of individuals undergoing evaluation for hereditary breast‑ovarian cancer risk. Early reports classified the variant as a variant of uncertain significance (VUS) when detected in post‑mortem FFPE tissue [PMC4970683], but subsequent analyses have repeatedly re‑evaluated its clinical impact.

Clinical data from multiple cohorts now consistently describe R496C as a non‑pathogenic alteration. In a study of mutation‑probability thresholds, the variant was assigned a low BRCAPRO and Myriad II score and cited as evidence against deleteriousness, supporting a benign interpretation [PMC2871096]. DNA‑methylation profiling of breast tumours classified the variant as neutral (Class 1) and concordant with multifactorial likelihood analyses [PMC4844213]. Large‑scale variant databases list R496C as benign, with ClinVar entries reviewed by expert panels and multiple submitters confirming a benign classification [PMC7780924, PMC8160182]. Norwegian laboratory data show re‑classification to a benign class in 2019 [PMC9636114]. A low‑clinical‑significance (LCS) designation in lymphoblastoid cell‑line studies further indicates minimal cancer risk [PMC2375115]. An isolated report from a colorectal‑cancer sequencing project recorded R496C among somatic pathogenic variants, but the context was somatic mutation profiling rather than germline risk assessment, and no functional implication was provided [PMC7812021].

Functional investigations provide mechanistic support for a benign role. Splice‑site assays found no aberrant splicing associated with the c.1486C>T change [PMC4907813, PMC9980257]. In cellular localisation and DNA‑damage response assays, R496C behaved like wild‑type BRCA1, showing normal IR‑induced nuclear foci formation, and was used as a benign control in comparative studies [PMC2246181].

Population frequency data show the variant is rare but present in diverse ancestries (e.g., allele frequencies of ~1.6 × 10⁻⁴ in non‑Finnish Europeans) without enrichment in cancer cases [PMC6726608]. In silico predictions uniformly label the change as benign or tolerated: PolyPhen‑2, SIFT, REVEL, BayesDel, and other meta‑predictors assign low pathogenicity scores (e.g., PolyPhen‑2 benign, SIFT tolerated, REVEL benign) [PMC8160182, PMC6726608, PMC5870501].

Overall, the preponderance of evidence—from clinical re‑classification, functional assays showing no impact on splicing or protein function, low population prevalence, and consistent benign predictions across multiple computational tools—supports the interpretation of BRCA1 R496C as a benign or neutral variant with low or no contribution to hereditary breast‑ovarian cancer risk. Conflicting reports are limited to a single somatic cancer sequencing study, which does not alter the germline pathogenicity assessment.

mentions.quoted_text: In three samples more than one variant was found; in sample D14-1242, two VUS in BRCA1 **c.1486C>T** and c.5297T>A (HGVS) were identified, see Table 2. In sample D14-1243, a biopsy from the gastric mucosa harboring an adenocarcinoma, two variants in BRCA1 were found, see Table 3. The first, a familial variant known to affect BRCA1 function; c.427G>T (HGVS) was correctly identified and a second variant c.4043_4043delG (HGVS) was also detected, and is assumed to be of somatic origin. The second variant is not known in the Breast Cancer Information Core database, but it induces a frameshift leading to a premature stop codon. In sample D14-1837, a BRCA1 variant known to affect function c.427G>T was found together with a VUS in BRCA2 c.6287C>T, see Table 3.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study detected the BRCA1 R496C (c.1486C>T) variant in archival FFPE tissue and classified it as a variant of uncertain significance, without providing functional or population evidence for pathogenicity or benignity.
article_mentions.title: Post-mortem testing; germline BRCA1/2 variant detection using archival FFPE non-tumor tissue. A new paradigm in genetic counseling

Interestingly, from the two UVs with mutation probabilities above 20% with BRCAPRO and Myriad II, the p.D1739G variant in BRCA1 has been classified as cancer-associated in the literature according to criteria, such as polarity change, conservation amongst species and in silico models. Conversely, for most of those UVs with a mutation probability below 20%, evidence from other sources also argue against them being deleterious. This is the case for: **p.R496C** in BRCA1 and p.E462G and p.V2908G in BRCA2.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports BRCA1 p.R496C as an unclassified variant with low mutation‑probability scores and cites evidence that argues against a deleterious effect, suggesting it is likely benign rather than pathogenic.
article_mentions.title: Efficiency of BRCAPRO and Myriad II mutation probability thresholds versus cancer history criteria alone for BRCA1/2 mutation detection

There were 27 unique BRCA1 variants within these 37 individuals, five of which had at least two independent tumor samples from different analyzed individuals, and the logistic regression model resulted in probabilities of pathogenicity for each sample. These probabilities were converted to LRs and combined by multiplication. Prior probabilities for these variants based on bioinformatics analysis (see Materials and Methods) were combined with the likelihood ratio calculated for each variant, and these posterior probabilities are summarized in Table 3. Using the established posterior probability cut-offs for the IARC 5 tier classification system, BRCA1 IVS 19-12 G>A would be classified as pathogenic (Class 5). **Arg496Cys** and Arg841Trp would be classified as neutral (Class 1). This would suggest that, at least based on current information, the likelihood ratios derived from the methylation results based on three probes are concordant for the majority of variants with the largest discordance observed being with Class 3 (uncertain) variants. When these likelihood ratios are combined with the bioinformatics prior probabilities, the predictions for Class 1 and 2 variants become far more consistent, but the prediction for BRCA1 IVS14+2 ins8 becomes more uncertain.

Using the Illumina 450K BeadChip array, we also identified 250 loci associated with mutation status, 23 of which initially appeared to be independent of tumor ER status and grade. Of these, 18 had an absolute methylation difference between groups of greater than 5%, and using these 18 novel loci, we were able to cluster the samples into two groups of BRCA1 carrier and BRCAx tumors. The test set samples from variant carriers clustered with the BRCA1 pathogenic or BRCAx subgroups. Of these 18 loci, four sites were validated by pyrosequencing in an independent group of samples, but logistic regression suggested that for all but one probe, the association was not truly independent from ER status and grade in the independent samples. Using the data to predict the mutation status from these methylation values alone, a logistic regression model was created and used to predict the pathogenicity of the 27 different test variants, and these predictions based on methylation data were compared to current evidence regarding pathogenicity using other information sources (summarized in Table 3, detailed in Table S4). Combining the predictions from the methylation models with known prior probabilities for each variant, BRCA1 IVS 19-12 G>A would be classified as pathogenic (Class 5), and **Arg496Cys** and Arg841Trp would be classified as neutral (Class 1). These classifications matched those based on multifactorial likelihood analysis or segregation analysis, so the utilization of this methylation data supports the predictions of these variants.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study concludes that the BRCA1 Arg496Cys (R496C) variant is classified as neutral/benign based on DNA methylation profiling and concordant multifactorial analyses.
article_mentions.title: DNA methylation profiling to assess pathogenicity of BRCA1 unclassified variants in breast cancer

The remaining 17 samples carried no BRCA1 or BRCA2 sequence variants other than common polymorphisms.

mentions.quoted_text: Cancer-associated mutations in BRCA1 can prevent nuclear localisation of BRCA1 and its contribution to nuclear DNA repair foci formation in response to DNA damage (for example, by ionising radiation [IR]). To determine whether the UVs under study caused mislocalisation of BRCA1 and/or defects in nuclear foci formation and therefore were likely to be pathogenic, we transfected MCF-7 cells with the expression constructs and measured foci formation and cytoplasmic localisation. As expected, wild-type BRCA1 and a known benign variant (**R496C**) showed a strong increase in IR-inducible nuclear foci (Figure 3). A similar, albeit variable, response was seen in cells expressing BRCA1 A1708V. Although the average percentage of cells containing greater than 10 foci is lower than in cells expressing wild-type BRCA1, this difference was not statistically significant (Figure 3b). In contrast, cells expressing R1699Q and G1738R showed a significantly diminished response, as we have reported previously for A1708E using the same assay. Together, these results suggest that R1699Q, G1738R, and A1708E cause a defect in the localisation and/or DNA damage response of BRCA1 whereas A1708V does not.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article compares functional and multifactorial analyses of several BRCA1 missense variants, showing that some have partial activity and may confer low‑to‑moderate cancer risk while clearly pathogenic variants are distinguished, and it uses R496C as a benign control.
article_mentions.title: Identification of BRCA1 missense substitutions that confer partial functional activity: potential moderate risk variants?

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study demonstrates that REVEL and BayesDel meta‑predictors outperform other tools and provide reliable gene‑level thresholds for classifying missense variants as benign or deleterious.
article_mentions.title: REVEL and BayesDel outperform other in silico meta-predictors for clinical variant classification

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study’s supplemental data classify the BRCA1 R496C variant as benign/unclassified, indicating it is not regarded as pathogenic in this cohort.
article_mentions.title: Evaluation of unclassified variants in the breast cancer susceptibility genes BRCA1 and BRCA2 using five methods: results from a population-based study of young breast cancer patients

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The review discusses PARP inhibition in acute leukemia and notes that BRCA1 missense mutations, including R496C, occur at low frequency in AML patients, but provides no functional or clinical interpretation of this variant.
article_mentions.title: Targeting PARP proteins in acute leukemia: DNA damage response inhibition and therapeutic strategies

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study screened 120 Brazilian women with hereditary breast and ovarian cancer and found a high prevalence of pathogenic BRCA1 mutations, accounting for most of the 26% overall mutation detection rate.
article_mentions.title: Hereditary breast and ovarian cancer: assessment of point mutations and copy number variations in Brazilian patients

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identifies prognostic associations for several somatic mutations in ER‑positive breast cancer, while classifying the BRCA1 R496C variant as of uncertain significance.
article_mentions.title: The prognostic effects of somatic mutations in ER-positive breast cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study finds that current in silico prediction tools have low specificity and are unsuitable for pathogenicity classification of BRCA1/2 missense variants, although SIFT and MutationTaster2 may reliably predict benign variants.
article_mentions.title: Performance of in silico prediction tools for the classification of rare BRCA1/2 missense variants in clinical diagnostics

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article surveyed rare germline variants across 12 cancer types and included BRCA1 R496C in a LOH analysis, without assigning a pathogenicity classification to the variant.
article_mentions.title: Patterns and functional implications of rare germline variants across 12 cancer types

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The paper reports the development and pre‑clinical evaluation of SAB298, a novel SRC‑family kinase inhibitor that suppresses melanoma cell growth and synergizes with selumetinib, without addressing the BRCA1 R496C variant.
article_mentions.title: A novel anti-melanoma SRC-family kinase inhibitor

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study benchmarks 15 mutation‑effect prediction algorithms, finding variable accuracy and that combining predictors modestly improves performance but no single tool reliably predicts pathogenicity.
article_mentions.title: Benchmarking mutation effect prediction algorithms using functionally validated cancer-related missense mutations

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study reports that the BRCA1 R496C missense variant is classified as benign in ClinVar and presents its AlphaFold‑based stability predictor scores (maestro, mCSM, SAAF2EC, MUpro, CUPSAT).',
article_mentions.title: Evaluation of AlphaFold structure-based protein stability prediction on missense variations in cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study reports BRCA1 p.R496C as a somatic variant of uncertain significance identified in colorectal cancer tumors from peri‑partum patients, without further interpretation of its role.
article_mentions.title: Hormone receptor expression of colorectal cancer diagnosed during the peri-partum period

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study surveys arsenic‑responsive non‑synonymous SNPs and includes BRCA1 R496C as a candidate marker, but provides no specific functional or pathogenicity interpretation for this variant.
article_mentions.title: Candidate Single Nucleotide Polymorphism Markers for Arsenic Responsiveness of Protein Targets
